headline: Once great - not anymore
pros: Experience working at a big bank or a big corporation gives one a very interesting perspective on the world.  Citigroup has, in the past, provided good opportunities for mobility and flexible work.  There is currently an initiative underway to promote alternative working strategies which is beneficial to those with families and long commutes.  As a junior candidate I've had the chance to work in a variety of different positions for a host of different managers and have truly developed many of my professional skills.  The compensation overall (prior to the current year) has been very decent as well.  Certain of the Senior Managers have also been very open and helpful in terms of career advice.
cons: It is a big corporation with a lot of inefficiencies.  For every experience where I have learned 1 thing to do, I have had a dozen which by observation I have learned what not to do.  The company is very layered with a lot of management and entering as a junior individual it is difficult to climb the corporate ladder here.  While many of your partners may recognize you and your achievements I have found that more often than not my own management has not expressed the same degree of recognition.  Lastly, having demonstrated competencies at a wide range of tasks I've found that I've been pidgeonholed into where there is need, even if my particular skill set can add more value at other things.

headline: Business Analyst
pros: Working for Deloitte provides you with a ton of experience in such a short period of time. Training and development are taken very seriously and a lot of time and effort is invested in each employee. The perks of traveling add up to free hotel nights, flights, and other reward programs you enroll yourself in. I hear bonuses are pretty amazing and the opportunity for growth is huge. Amazing benefits.
cons: All of the pros come at a price. In my experience, I was traveling to my client site for a year Sunday nights to Friday nights- I was only home for a 24 hour period each week and required to work 7 AM to midnight most nights. I was typically also required to work weekends and was never able to take a single holiday during my time nor any PTO. Specific to my team, I wasn't even allowed to have a say in what I ate for lunch or dinner due to tight timelines- one member of the team would decide where they were going and have to grab lunch for everyone. Even though I was given 5 weeks of PTO, in my entire time there I only used 2 weeks and got a huge payout when I left. The environment is very competitive and not collaborative at all and favoritism is rampant. Some people live for this kind of life and culture and a lot of my team members enjoyed it. However, I value being able to spend time with family, have work/life balance, and work among people who are collaborative and supportive. This was not the environment for me.

headline: A company that adapts, but must do so more quickly

The business is doing very well in acquiring huge customers (Amazon, Wal-mart, Citigroup), and the stock price has gained 40% in the past year. Our product will be transformed with Prism Analytics and Cloud Platform.
cons: The almost leisurely work-life balance can lead to inertia from those who take advantage of the situation. This affects our release velocity of features and products. Career progression is often determined not by your accomplishments, but by who you know and who recognizes you.  Application developers face a career-limiter in our proprietary coding language. Compensation is below industry-standards, even when compared to other large B2B such as Salesforce and Microsoft.

headline: Great if you're young, single and a Type A personality
pros: Great company, great products, fun co-workers, usually okay customers, decent pay for retail. Customers can really get to know you, which is good for some people, bad for others. I made lifelong friends while working there. Slight employee discount, which isn't better than the Student Discount they do once a year. It's easy to get caught up in living in the ever present hype of the Apple machine, which can be really fun and exciting. It's also nice to always meet new people every day. It's nice selling products that really live up to their promise.
cons:
Not a family friendly place to work. You're required to work one weekend day, meaning you never get a full weekend with your family, if you have one. The shifts range in start times. Great pay for retail, but in the grand scheme of things, and considering what Apple brings in, it's pretty low for what they expect from you. You're supposed to always be 'on' and be an encyclopedia of Apple knowledge, which is fine. Just know that going in. You're on stage before you even enter the store, in the break rooms, at lunch, and even when you're walking to your car. 

One big deal is that it's a very uncomfortable place to work, physically. It looks like a spaceship, and is about as ergonomic. The chairs for the tables are horrible, and the slate floors are murder for full time shifts. Even the break rooms are cramped, noisy, highly trafficked, and uncomfortable. 

They're also Draconian in their confusing point system for tardiness and absences. It's done in so many great employees. It seems to be MUCH more important than the quality of their staff. They'd rather have mediocre staff with perfect attendance than amazing people who are a little late now and then. Example: you get a point if you're more than three minutes late. You can be let go very easily. So if you live on flex-time, Apple isn't going to work out for you. You're only allowed to Think Different when you're not on the clock. 

There's also very few places to go in the company once you're in, which isn't bad for most people, but it's definitely not a way to go further with Apple beyond their retail structure.

headline: I was warned off the Co-op but took a risk, don't do the same.
pros: Very few pros except that you get a few discounts on Co-op food and other areas. The Angel square building is nice but the CIS Tower had mice.
cons:
This was by far the biggest career mistake I have ever made. I left a job to come to Co-op, however the practices that went on were so shocking that I would rather be unemployed than continue to work there. Firstly, the building was beyond repair (CIS tower) and should be demolished. There were mice, broken furniture everywhere and unsanitary conditions - no kitchen sinks so people used to leave sponges in the toilets and wash their mugs, plates, etc there. Secondly, however much the Co-op wants to say it's changed and is 'back to being co-op' it simply hasn't. The culture was shocking; people being wrongly dismissed, illegal practices, contractors being kept on for 3 - 4 years at a time on obscene day rates yet being asked to interview their permanent replacements (is it any surprise they delay filling these roles as much as possible?). Thirdly, the systems are so appalling that customer data is routinely at risk, so much so that I have ceased even being a customer of Co-op. 
I was advised by a few people (including ex employees and recruiters) not to move to the co-op and I ignored them, I really wish I hadn't made that mistake.

My experience was that once you are hired, you will be given much more than what you were hired to do with promise of opportunity. You might do an excellent job but management is only looking out for themselves and how good you make them look. After working at a site for several years and myself receiving excellent referrals from the client, our site lost it's contract. We were told after a couple of months we could look for other work and it was okay to say we had lost our contract by management and our site leader. Upon being offered another position elsewhere and accepting , CBRE refused to pay me my merit raise with retro pay before I left,  which I had earned for work performance evaluated for the previous year. Also, when my new supervisor called to ask for assistance with documentation , he was told by my previous CBRE management that I had left and they had not lost their contract. They flat out lied to my new employer,  because I had copies of company emails sent out by corporate which made CBRE really look ridiculous to my new employer since I had proof. Management with no financial skills are put in charge of site leadership and finances are completely out of control.If you are given tasks to do by upper management, even though you may get some support for work well done, you may not realize it, but my experience is that somebody other than you, usually someone in management will take the credit for it. I myself had ownership name taken off of important documents and changed to a managers name. I put in months and hours of work on work in sensitive presentations for training and had it presented as the presenter. Upon exiting my employment with the company and later having to follow up with CBRE to begin my new job, I found out by my ex previous supervisor that they had gone to coworkers and discussed me and asked them how  to complete my exit documents and assist in new hire with my company instead of going to HR. Imagine having your supervisor discussing your personal HR questions with not other supervisors or HR, but with another company and coworkers. So many bad decisions and extremely bad choices made by poor leadership because people weren't hired by their knowledge or experience. When you put people in charge who don't know what they are doing there are going to be OSHA recordables( many that occurred but weren't reported by management) , issues with budget mainly poor spending by management, people working under management who are going to be put at risk because of poor management skills and a strain of many other issues. You have people who are put on jobs by the person who assigns the tasks in the system, and issues 100 tickets a month , putting two people to a ticket, making it look like there is work....to screw in one light bulb. THAT is fact. Their systems aren't audited or clearly not read over. The list goes on and on.........

headline: Support Engineer
pros:
- We can begin between 8am and 10am and finish between 4pm and 6pm. 
- Free food but the quantity is limited. 
- Gym onsite with classes and a funny instructor. 
- Two days working from home every week. However, you cannot work from home neither Fridays nor Mondays. 
- When you work on a Saturday or Sunday, you get 1,5days in exchange (instead of 1 day as other companies do). You get 2 days in exchange when you work a bank holiday. 
- The pension plan is really good in comparison with other companies. If you put 4% of your salary and the company adds a 10%. Other companies only match what you put.
- You can create a development plan and get advice from HR. I have seen people moving to other departments but have also seen people trying hard and didn´t succeed. It all depends how helpful your manager is.
When a person in my team tried to move to another department, his current manager said to the future manager, “we need him here. We cannot afford losing him from our team”. This person eventually left the company.
- You can charge your electric car (if you have one) for free.
cons:
- Several People in the team have suffered burnouts. Since January this year SAP is not replacing people who left the team. This means than the ones staying have double of work and end up suffering burnouts.
- The salaries are completely unfair. People who began years ago were offered less salary than the ones beginning nowadays. Despite all the salaries reviews done every March, some salaries are still far away from being at the same level. There are cases with €3000 of difference between the new and old people. The best is that you cannot talk about these differences with your manager because it is written in the contact that you cannot say your salary to anybody. If you try to point out this difference, they will try to force you to say the name of the person and this employee will get in trouble. 
- The salary increases really little. Sometimes the salary increase is €450 gross a year!
- You always have to queue to go to the toilet in the main building. There are really few toilets for the number of people working there.
- The main building is an eco-friendly building. This mean that sometimes is warm and sometimes is cold. I still remember when everybody was sent home during the irish heat wave last year. There were only 30ºC outside but we all felt like chicken being roasted inside an oven in the main building.

They will lie to you every chance they get, they lose a lot of good talent because they turned this place into a sweatshop. Prior to May 6th 2020 this was a more relaxed environment and they didn’t slave drive like they do now. Once the department went from operations to Wealth management the entire dept went to crap. when you have an issue management just tells you to deal with it instead of finding a solution. The entire service metrics are totally out of wack ex. If you’re 1min late you’re technically 2mins late with how they track things. Since you’re 1 min off and 1min not logged in it’s scored against adherence as 2mins. So if you go over a long time on a phone call into your break you’re screwed. I never worked for her but Lindsey is an absolute joke of a manager and should be fired immediately. All I hear her talk about is how drunk she gets when she’s not working. Really the only person who knows what’s going on there is Linda. Don’t work here if you can help it. I was lucky I found a much much better job and left.

Majority of the time it'll be this elaborate unfortunate turn of events that was out of his/her hands or control, and just being able to tell them & let them feel like they're not my customer, they really are a valued opportunity to be able to come up with a solution where they felt better when they got off the phone. Because always, with the angry customers you can almost hear when the stress/ worries begin to fade away and they are just hearing everything you're saying because it would just benefit them in their situation.

headline: Outdated and collapsing digital function trapped under the thumb of a corporate giant
pros: =- there are Some truly brilliant People still present who are a million miles ahead of the rest of the company
cons:
Culture:
The digital function has slowly, surely and successfully managed to replace a broad spectrum of bright engineering talent with a large and pointless upper crust of managers and to see such a modern and free-flowing function be slowly torn apart and picked clean by corporate politicking is, quite frankly, depressing. 

Technology and Innovation:
Technological development is hamstrung by the physical behemoth of the existing bricks and mortar corporate side causing innovation to have to go through the trials of Heracles to even stand a chance at being considered whilst napkin ideas from higher ups with no technological expertise are forced upon the development cycle without any pushback from those with the power to do so. 

Trust:
The lack of trust of developers within the function is outstanding. Hiring professionals in a field and then dictating how to do their job speaks volumes about how the now enormous managerial line have no idea how to manage developers, no idea how software development actually works and worst of all are not willing to learn; instead preferring to simply apply what I can into describe as production line like draconian measures in place. 

Loyalty and Morality:
Whilst it should come as no surprise to anyone other than the naive that loyalty to a company is folly if you expect any slither of such from management here you will be solely disappointed. To say that people are just a number there is putting it lightly, and given the high demand for developers in this current climate is something that needs to be addressed as the number one priority before the already existing haemorrhaging of people becomes irrevocable.

cons:
Big discrepancy between Boston (HQ) and Lab (Austria + Poland) on what products value proposition is.  This can be validated by the recent push to a "DPM" model as opposed to what customers/lab is developing for (improve SDLC)
Although many positive changes were made once the company went private, many legacy senior managers still remain in place.  These individuals are keeping certain aspects of the company of maturing, and have a track record of negatively impacting the organizations they lead
No equity opportunity for top performers outside of a one time purchasing option that was riddled with issues and questions
Compensation is towards the low to mid-level for similar organizations
Performers appear to not be valued, failures appear to have no repercussions

headline: Good company to work for, treats employees well
pros:
The basics are done well, clear pay structure and bonus for all staff, development opportunity's are there if you aim for them and show your qualities. Training for staff is clear and structured. A huge benefit is the company pension scheme, it's excellent and shows in my opinion a degree of class that is highlighted by company's infrastructure. 

Having worked for other large optical multiples over 15 years I can honestly say my time with Boots Opticians so far has been a rewarding experience. There consistency of approach with there people is the real highlight of my time so far. As a Manager yes the buck stops and starts with you but that's Management, you have the autonomy to build and develop your own team and aim to deliver business objectives set by company and for the Boots brand whist leading customer healthcare as a focus at all times.

A very pleasant alternative in comparison to the model set by other optical multiples who's focus is generally led by profit sharing with partnership creating in my opinion an inconsistent message and poor environment for employees that varies hugely from practice to practice depending on owners personal motivation.
cons:
Where the company needs to improve is investing in its Optometry Personnel. There seems to be a "fear" of making the commitment of taking the established optometrists out of their test room environment and developing them into people leaders of there top stores. There is a huge wealth of knowledge contained within these personnel with all the skills already there to be developed further with ease but for some reason nobody ever seems to act on it properly. Instead the general approach tends to be to invest in external retail based candidates or pharmacy colleague's to lead and manage the practice's. All that really does is create a lack of empathy and understanding between colleagues.  " it's easier to teach an optometrist retail than a retailer optometry "

There is a real opportunity to invest in optometrists career journey in this company and differentiate themselves from there competitors as employer of choice. They are already beating others with pay structure and clinical environment, if they nail this they will be onto something special. 

P.s. I am not an optometrist

headline: My time at lv
pros:
The people, I have met some great people whilst working at lv=
The day I left.
cons:
Where do I start 
-Unsupportive team leaders- was having a bad week one week due to a family accident and team leader heard me discussing this with a colleague, a few hours or day later I received an email which I felt was written in not a pleasant manner moaning about my wrap time. 
-Also had problems with inputting my holiday hours into the system and another tl who was looking at this kept rejecting it as it was put in incorrectly. On one occasion the said tl rejected it and then a few minutes later sent an aggressive email back which was not helpful when I did not know what I was doing. This upset me to the point I had to take a comfort break and went to the toilet and I remember shedding tears. I then reported this to my tl at the time and got him to read the email. It seemed to go over his head so I brought it up again in my 121 my tl said next time this happens come to me which indicated he did not do anything when I reported this email originally. 
-I received a customer complimentary email one time, my tl at the time did not acknowledge this but instead moaned at me for not clapping in his team meetings. 
- I felt I was made a scapegoat for something that should not have been happening before I started working at lv= and the management team were aware of this and it happened to me when I first started and no one said anything or said this was wrong.Yet when I did the said thing and someone else complained I got put on a disciplinary and got a written warning for it which I felt was unfair. I did not appeal this as at that point I had lost all trust in the management team at lv= and this is what led me to leaving.

- I also felt that tl’s had their favourites and if you were not one of them you were held back and when mentioned the management team denies this even if mentioned by several people.

headline: The more time goes on the happier I am with the company
pros:
- Great, supportive management (in my case anyway) with regular catchups to see how you're progressing
- Streamlined promotion process with clear competencies and expectations outlined to help you progress.
-Company listens to employees- conducts an annual anonymous survey with feedback for the leadership team and management. Last year invested in learning and development, this year invested in benefits and are trying to be more transparent with strategy as a result of feedback.
- Good benefits (pension matching, booking.com discount, €300 to spend online, bonuses)
- Modern tech stack (in my bit of the business- see cons)
- Always pursuing better engineering practices (with input from devs)
-Mostly relaxed and fun culture- no dress code and you can joke around with colleagues (obviously within reason, but I've yet to offend anybody), parties twice a year with fun events for christmas, halloween etc
- Good work/life balance encouraged, focus on good mental health. Managers don't think you're a better employee just because you work longer hours, I was discouraged from it by one manager because they don't want people to burn out.
cons:
- The internal IT services can be pretty meh. The internet connectivity can be a bit iffy, and we run into problems with accessing different accounts (linked learning, email, safari books)- internet issues can block you for an hour or so max, the access stuff can be fixed within a couple of hours if you report it
- State of the codebase/tech stack varies depending on what part of the business you work in- it's good in my area, bad in others.
-You don't get pay raises (aside from adjustments for inflation) unless you're promoted, but they do regularly promote people who meet the competencies
- In my bit of the business we've had some team instability, mine have been changed around every six months or so (when a lead/product owner leaves, or new ones start, teams can be merged together or split up). Whenever a team changes they do try to keep people together though.
-Culture can be a touch corporate at times with ethics meetings and the like. Sometimes feel there isn't much of a social aspect (get togethers, team building) but that partially depends on the team/department.

headline: A great place to learn people skills and coping skills.

Advancement opportunities seem to be limited only by your willingness to step up.
cons: Fast food can be surprisingly stressful; many customers think lowly of food workers and will be condescending, and they will often act as though you have intentionally targeted them when mistakes happen.  Being on your feet all day in a relatively fast paced environment is exhausting.  Your pay is based on the simplicity of the job, but it often is not enough to compensate for the stress.  It takes a special patience and truly unconditional love for the general population to actually want to pursue a career in McDonald's.

headline: Competitive Environment with very Smart Co-Workers
pros: Everyone can agree that the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation does world changing work.  I have worked there in two different capacities two different times in the last 9 years and the culture of highly competitive, extremely intelligent people remain.  The company still operates as a young organization in that it embraces new ideas and it's always evolving.  People who dislike change would not be happy here.  The benefits can't be beat and the environment is stunning in the new campus.  They encourage working from home and remote work, which is helpful in the sometimes intense and stressful office environment.
cons: This is a company of academics, there is not a lot of silliness or fun happening at this organization, which makes sense due to the importance of their mission.  There is a competitive feeling throughout the company that can feel oppressive, but they still embrance a sense of creativity and change.  They constantly re-org often with little to no notice or reasoning behind the changes which is pretty jarring, so you have to be someone who can embrance change easily to thrive in this environment.

headline: Stellar firm, good exit opportunities
pros:
1. High-impact project work with room for rapid advancement
2. **Very** transparent (and competitive) compensation model 
3.  Potential for industry specialization and content acquisition early on (Oliver Wyman's focus on a smaller set of industries creates deep industry expertise)
cons:
Caveat: this is the only place I have worked so I do not have many points of comparison.

The most impactful cons of Oliver Wyman are the same as those at any management consulting firm (long hours, tough travel schedules, uneven project work and at times inexperienced project managers)

Specific potential cons of OW:
1. Sometimes frustrating staffing model and very opaque staffing process leads to inefficient outcomes
2. Inexperienced middle management (i.e. project managers) - since Oliver Wyman has no defined "up or out" at 2 years time, the third and fourth year managers are sometimes inexperienced (potentially a bit burned out without a 2 year break for MBA)
3. Less formal/impactful MBA support - likely the biggest driver of my decision to leave Oliver Wyman

headline: EY is a solid employer
pros: EY has really great benefits including unlimited paid time off, a wellness fund worth $1000, free therapy, and more. EY also provides all of its employees with a councilor to talk through what career path you want to follow or any work related concerns. There are lots of growth options and your councilor is transparent about what you need to do to achieve those goals.
cons: EY is overwhelmingly large. I am currently on a project with more than 300 people and I feel that I do not get noticed or recognized for the work I do. There is a lack of connection between upper management and those activity working in production on the project and the concerns of the workers seem to never be acknowledged.

headline: British Airways
pros: There are opportunities to work in many different areas of the company from front line operations, to commercial sales, to cargo, to loyalty, to customer experience and project management. cons: As with any large company, it can be a labyrinth to navigate. We have also had some conflicts with our unionized workforce that has created deep seated resentments on both sides of the issues.

headline: A good place if you are young, single and want to make career in research (or already have a career))
pros: World-leading research, lots of interesting projects, quite a lot of them are from industry (so it's not THAT detached from real life). Staff and students are friendly and helpful and when they are not there is a very good support scheme from the uni to setlle such problems. Good pension scheme, own sports centre with the gym and swimming pool (though often packed with students), lots of extracurricular activities such as clubs and sports sections. Own nursery (though not free). Free unlimited travel insurance cover (if travel on Colege business). Can keep flexible hours (work from home sometimes or arrive later/leave earlier if needed).
cons: Very bad work-life balance. As other reviewers said, expectations are very high and work over weekends and around the clock is a norm here. Mentions of bad working conditions in some departments are also true (rooms are badly lit, stuffy, tiny, overcrowded. Not all of them of course, depends on the building). Salaries are small for London (and for this workload)

pros: It is a good working experience if you enjoy working with foreign trade etc. There are a lot of departments and everyone should have a chance to work within or be familiar with them. They have so many worldwide locations so the chances of one ending up in a different city are rare to none, it is disappointing, they should have more cross training amongst departments and ability to go to another office if one desires

You have to sit deskside to someone who has been here for six months or more, its kind of annoying if that person is in a senior position because they have that ego booster already and they have been here for too long so its hard to get adjusted to that. Vacation time is only 2 weeks no matter what tenant position you hold, three weeks after 12 months should be given as most companies do anyways. MORE VACATION TIME!

headline: Good work, but it doesn't last.
pros:
Fellow employees.  A Bachelors degree minimum is required to work as a Pearson "Peak Employee," as they are known,  so everyone around you has a  level of education higher than the average population.  They know what I'm talking about, and I know what they're talking about.
Low level of stress (most of the time) - you score achievement tests all day.
Interesting mix of states - Pearson contracts with different state's departments of education to score that state's tests.  Every state is slightly different in the way they want their tests scored.
cons: Job is temporary.  Facility is open and operational only as long as Pearson has tests to score, which is not the entire year.  When the kids are out of school, and before the next year's tests start to come in (July thru Oct), there is no work.  Work through the school year is sporadic, though management IS trying to bring work in to fill up the whole year.

headline: Excellent compensation and benefit
pros:
If you can obtain a permanent position, the pay and benefit are among the best in USA for the same level of education and experience. How many other companies give you $50K+ starting salary as a fresh college grad, plus full health insurance, matching 401K, annual bonus and 3 weeks of paid vacation? As you progress higher, the benefit actually gets better. At my level Bayer basically gives double the salary of my previous local employer! 

Other pros:
- The work hours are reasonably flexible if you have a good boss, and fortunately mine is. Certain locations even have summer hours.
- While in most other companies "people are our most valuable asset" is an empty slogan, Bayer puts it into policy and action. There is a corporate-wide compensation committee that determines the appropriate level of pay for each new position, and their philosophy is to offer a competitive package in order to get the best person for the job and minimize his propensity to jump elsewhere.
- As a health care company personnel health and wellness receive special attention. One of your tasks as a new hire is to make sure your work environment is ergonomically optimal.
cons:
In many ways it is still a traditional company with a lot of bureaucracy and silos. Your ability to accomplish most tasks would be limited by your system authorization, which means you have to ask for help from people in other roles or departments.

Other cons:
- There is a lot of top-down counterproductive decision making. Somebody in Germany would arbitrarily decide on a standard, then strongly resist any attempt to change it even when faced with a clear customer requirement against it.
- Older employees who don't wish to or incapable of learning new methods and technologies are kept, then distributed around many departments. It is great for them, but not great for others who want to continuously improve the company when faced with real market / costumer challenge.

headline: Be a cog
pros: There are enough free food, amenities, and parties that you never need leave campus.  Everybody is friendly.  Lots of things to work on.
cons: All housing near HQ is priced for the top wage earners at Google so you can't buy a house and renting will eat your entire paycheck.  Google's coding environment is designed for the lowest common denominator and twisted to support a nearly infinitely large codebase.  95% of your time will be waiting for spaghetti code to build, fighting code review trolls, following Google code style, trying to figure out bone-headed non-standard architectures, debugging your Google-modified world, wading through endless obsolete documents, and dealing with the politics of egomaniacs pushing their dumb new framework/storage/language/whatever in an attempt to gain recognition within a nearly infinite megacorp.  Google uses almost nothing from the outside world so almost nothing learned can be applied to your career elsewhere.  There are insufferable KoolAid guzzlers saying they love the "startup culture," as if it was normal to write 100 lines of code in a day, wait a week for it to be committed, and then attend a party of 1000 strangers texting while a computerized DJ blasts retro pop music.  For all of this effort, regardless of the official project title, what you're really doing is enabling the creepy collection of marketing data.

headline: Genuinely Care About Employees!
pros:
I've been at Salesforce for several years now after coming from one of the big 4 consulting firms, and it's been night and day! Although I was a top performer at my previous company, it was clear that I was disposable to them regardless and they would rather hire someone with less experience than try to retain me (my requests were not related to salary, but changes to projects and overall happiness). I provided a lot of feedback on how to improve processes and promote collaborative culture as other coworkers were feeling similarly, but nothing was done.

At Salesforce, I completely feel as if I'm heard by management. I've transferred to several different teams with ease (in fact, after one year at the company they encourage it and create programs to shadow other roles). My managers are genuinely interested in progressing my career and enabling me to succeed and I've enjoyed working with all the teams I've been on.

I've had a lot of flexibility to ensure I still have a nice work/life balance and that I actually get to use all the perks available! I personally use all of the $100 gym membership stipend (you can use it for massages too if you don't work out!) and the generous VTO balance (56 hours to volunteer at my local animal shelter, yes please!).
cons: There's a learning curve to trying to not become too corporate, but at the same time 19,000+ employees globally. Occasionally I feel that we are very top heavy management wise.

headline: Sage needs to step up!
pros: The benefits were nice.  The people I worked with were outstanding.
cons: The travel required for the position was simply unsustainable.  Implementing software solutions and having to travel 95%, constantly being pulled in 20 directions.  I had 5 managers in my region alone that had control of my schedule and when another manager from a different region would request assistance, they too would have control of my schedule, only adding to the confusion. Conflicts with schedules due to overlaping of time were common.  They could plug-in a new project on my calendar and send me off with out consulting me or even asking if it would be a burden on my family.  The consideration for "quality of life" was very poor, the organization of management was fractured and disorganized and the lack of a consistent corporate vision only added to the frustration and confusion.  Fore example, in 5+ years with the company we changed our name 5 times.  (Medical Manager, MM Health Systems, WebMD, Emdeon Health Systems, Sage Software Health Division).  The communication between departments was poor, the employee moral was poor, senior leadership rarely interacted with the employees in the field and the career opportunities for advancement were almost non-existent.  Once you're in the field, it's very hard to get off the road and move into an office position.

headline: No Respect for Employees
pros: I can't really think of anything. Some groups might have good immediate management.
cons:
Oracle has so many problems, I don't know where to start. They don't express their appreciation for employees. Raises are very rare.

The hiring process at Oracle is impossible. All new-hires go through the CEO's offices and can take months. This means that only the least-employable people get hired. In addition, the company likes to hire new-college grads and pay them more than existing engineers. They tend to stay for 2 years, get lots of coaching from senior engineers, and then leave for greener fields. This leaves frustrated, underpaid engineers behind.

I had faith in the upper management's ability to look at the long-term goals. This ended when Oracle killed off Solaris and Sparc development. They have some idea of focusing on the cloud, but killed the only differentiation they had. Now they will be racing Amazon and Google to the bottom on Cloud offerings. I was particularly amused that they killed Sparc development right before Meltdown and Spectre undercut Intel/AMD.

headline: Not the work culture you would expect from a charity
pros: Work for a charity that helps older people. Good work related opportunities can be available depending on your locality and manager.
cons: There can be subtle bullying, harassment and victimisation that takes place. Usually the line managers are the cause of this problem and it is very difficult to raise it. Big disparity within the organisation for working environments and development. Organisation has a business arm and a charity arm. There is no trade union recognised for the business arm also limited work experience or training opportunities offered for the business. Employee are not usually recognised for the quality of work you do but whether you "fit in" or get on well with management. Progression is very dependent on how 'friendly' you are with the managers. Diversity appears to be very limited with who is employed. Disputes at work are not handled sensitively and even more difficult to have your side heard and reach a reasonable resolution. Raising concerns can result in the employee made to feel worse which is why so many don't bring it up. Recent Third Sector article has finally shed light on this side of things.

headline: Advise against re-badging into IBM during IT outsourcing
pros:
My company outsourced their IT to IBM, and as a staff member in the IT department, I was hired in to IBM for support of the account ("re-badged").  While it wasn't a horrible experience, I would not advise anyone to follow this path.

For pros - IBM is a massive company, and one can theoretically move towards an IT career with limitless  growth and opportunities in every technology sector.  During my time with IBM I was treated fairly and my manager was reasonable and cordial.  I worked long hours but was compensated as an hourly employee.
cons:
As a re-badged employee there was constant tension between IBM goals and the goals of previous co-workers who were now on the other side of the fence.

I expected to be able to tap into the vast wealth of knowledge that a massive corporation like IBM might offer, but in reality each account was largely an island in terms of support, and getting assistance from resources assigned to other accounts was very burdensome where possible at all. 

I expected that a technology company would be on the forefront of that field, but in almost every regard I found that only the most basic system administrator practices were understood and employed by IBM.  Battling with IBM management against cookie-cutter designs which conflicted with customer requirements, or outright didn't solve the customer's problem, was a regular and frustrating occurrence.

Despite significantly increased responsibilities and being at the near-bottom of the pay scale, annual salary adjustment was embarassing (<1% despite being rated as an exceptional employee).  Same story on the bonus front (my bonus didn't make it into the triple digits, <$100).

headline: Difficult, but doable.
pros:
-Great People: Both the managers and AE's create a really fun work environment. Most of the directors and the senior director are approachable, knowledgable and interested in the success of their employees. Obviously, I can't say this for everyone in the office, but for the most part, I genuinely enjoy my co-workers, bosses, and support staff.
-Great Product: Once you get down to the actual selling of the product, it's not that difficult to do. Indeed offers an excellent product, and it makes the job fairly simple.
-Work/Life Balance: You aren't expected to stay late or come in on weekends. As long as you're working hard while at the office, no one expects you to stay late.
-Bonuses/Commission: Great bonus structure. You have the opportunity to make a lot of money.
cons:
-Training: The product itself is complicated, not to mention the home-grown CRM. The week-long training in CT is confusing, and for the most part AE's have to re-learn everything when they get to their home offices. Also, the person who conducts the sales trainings has never been in sales. 
-Lead-Gen: There is none. Don't come into the role expecting for leads to be handed to you. Plenty of AE's (myself included) do the job, find leads and hit numbers; it's not impossible by any means. Some say that the market is over-saturated, and while it is definitely difficult to find leads, it's also part of the job description, so I don't necessarily think it's a justifiable complaint. 
-No Room for Growth: I think that this is the primary reason for the turnover. Most of the talented and driven employees leave simply because they are ready for a bigger opportunity, and Indeed doesn't have any available. 

Bottom Line: This is a sales job without lead generation. The people are great, the product is awesome, but the job is not for those without a strong work-ethic.

cons: Hard work and long hours, especially for management-level folks who do the proposal writing and backstop delivery. Occasional bait-and-switch with bonuses. Partners are stretched so thin that they are unavailable for important meetings. Lots of travel (could be a pro for some). Career paths are not well-defined (especially for those with superior technical skills) and it takes a lot of determination to get the guidance and training needed to advance and keep skills sharp. Budgets for external training are poorly defined and approvals are completely discretionary, which makes it difficult for staff-level personnel to get anything other than internal training.

headline: Nepotism Rules.
pros: The service users and fellow support workers. They are the only reason I have stayed there for the last seven years.
cons: Senior support workers and higher are corrupt. The only way to make it beyond support worker is to be equally as corrupt. If you question anything, they threaten you with your job. They laugh unions out the door. I have been suspended three times for things I have not done, especially if I've gone up against a manager. Rather than investigate what really happened in an incident, their immediate reaction is to cover and provide an alibi for the manager in question. Especially in several cases of abuse I have bore witness to. Workers are regularly threatened with their jobs, stating that support workers are easily replaced. I've been threatened several times for whistleblowing. And by Anna Luntz herself. I am also being forced to work 48 hours over Xmas eve, day and boxing day, my contract is 30 hours and I did not want overtime, but apparently my manager wanted Xmas off so I'm being forced to do her hours too. We are also using paperwork that has not been reviewed in nearly ten years and are told to 'bait' our service user into fights so that Creative Support don't lose the funding for extra Citrus trained staff if service users are getting too well behaved, stating "it's not financially beneficial to us for them to get better".

headline: More than decent overall if you don't mind about ethics (or lack of)
pros:
- Good compensation and benefits
- Paid holidays
- Very flexible work from home policy
- Easy to dig your hole due to short-sighted resource management - after 6 months in, you're already a subject matter expert on several subjects.
Overall it's a decent deal, but only if you're able to put up with the numerous cons, which you won't be able to if you have ethics (but then you wouldn't be working in a bank in the first place).
cons:
Local level:
- Business owners are reactive rather than proactive. Constant fire drill/band-aid mode, no long term vision, stuff gets scraped before it's even completed. Wasteful.
- This in turn causes lots of overtime, which BTW is unpaid.
- Business owners are haughty and disrespectful.
- Red tape is out of control, even after factoring in the strong auditing needs.
- Short-sighted resource management - most tech resources are offshore and have a very high turnover rate (some are gone as soon as they've been trained - clever if you ask me). Can't blame them when you see how they're treated.
- Reliance on consulting firms to "help" with business decisions.
- The Atrium building in Buffalo is by far the worst office I've ever worked in. No personal space, noisy environment, sub-par canteen with very limited outside options, undersized restrooms, surveillance cameras in the actual work area...
Global level:
- Known money launderer - all banks probably are, but not many are bad enough to get caught...
- Corporate culture is all "Do as I say, not as I do" - tone at the top doesn't match what's required of you in the mandatory propaganda sessions
- Too much middle management being of seemingly no use, except leading political battles against each other (common in big companies I guess)

headline: Worst company I ever worked for
pros:
The only positives I can think of are that some of the people deserve better.

The coffee van every morning.
cons:
It's by far the worst place I ever worked at and other people seem to have experienced this in other locations too. 

As an engineer I was grossly undervalued and never acknowledged for the work I did which ultimately made me leave in the end. I felt mistreated and never recognised for the hard work I was doing. 

They also grossly underpay their employees. Not just underpay but grossly underpay! We had a 1% pay rise in a few years... when you factor in inflation it wasn't a pay rise at all. I've more than doubled my annual salary since I left Equiniti which tells you all you need to know. I can't give a view on other roles, but as an engineer avoid Equiniti at all costs.

Another thing which is a shambles is reporting to a 'team leader' who, in any other company, would be a junior developer at most. They are young and inexperienced but have been there since they graduated. This smacks of loyalty and having favourites. 

They also moved offices to one which had many plumbing issues and an incredibly small car park (17 spaces or so) which had to be shared with another company (I'd say 80+ people in total). This was a shambles. Why would they do this??

headline: Administrator
pros:
I've been working here for 10+yrs. I came into the business with no knowledge of the  Optics industry, so everything I have learned has come from Specsavers. They seem keen to allow people who have no previous experience to join the team., and invest in training and education.

Job security,  I know that I won't just be cut loose, I've never known anyone to be made redundant in the time I've been there.

Company attitude to value and quality of service and products is laudable but is let down by individuals within the team.

It's very easy to turn up, do my job and leave at the end of each day.
cons:
There are so many levels of inequality.  Only team members SEEN to be making money for the business are rewarded with any kind of bonus. None of the supporting staff is considered valuable and there is a culture of not following tasks through to completion and assuming that the support staff are happy to pick up the loose ends. I myself spend 40-50% of my working week tying up unfinished or sloppy work, some of those staff can be paid 5x what I am!!

People who have been there a long time and who have made no effort to establish a high standard of work are simply allowed to continue with their sloppy attitudes but if you raise your own standards at any time you are punished for not meeting those standards regularly, so those who don't bother are simply allowed or encouraged to coast through. This has led to different standards/rules being applied to different people.

The culture among the employees is very clicky, you have to be a confident strong personality to survive, let alone win any popularity contest, only popular staff get a collection at their birthdays or when going on maternity leave or leaving the company. I have never had a birthday whip round.

Customers get a better deal on discounted products than staff!!

Communication of information to the staff is appalling. At this time the opening hours are changing in less than a month and we haven't been officially told, I found out by pure mistake.

You don't really know who is senior and who isn't, and there are far too many members of the management team!

We are required to give 6 weeks notice for booking time off (fair enough) but we have to attend meetings out of hours with 2 weeks notice. Yet another example of double standards.

Nothing ever changes. When failures in processes are identified, they are looked at and changed for a few weeks then old habits come back!

It's pot luck whether you get to someone in IT who knows what they are doing.

We know very little about the CEO so I can't comment on him.

headline: WORST PLACE TO WORK, THINK TWICE BEFORE GETTING A JOB HERE!!
pros: The benefits such as half price current account, share match, salary and pension!
cons: There is not a single nice thing I can say about Lloyds, just think very carefully before working here. Its a bullying culture and the managers dont care about their staff at all. They put huge amounts of pressure to sell, even though no targets. They expect you to work overtime for free, and in a lot of cases not getting out until 6.30pm but only paid until 5pm. They support mental health, yet make staff suffer in silence. Too many middle managers that want to recieve big bonus`s! They expect you to turn up at 8.30 am and be ready for a meeting at 8.45am, however not paid until 9am. If you turn up after 8.45am they`ll leave you on the doorstep as punishment. Service calls are a nightmare- if you dont get a good one then there is a huge witch hunt from middle managers to find out who is to blame for not getting top scores, making staff scared to death to get a service call. sadly, all the bad practices from 12 years ago are coming back and I fear it will be another slap on the hand from the FCA and maybe more fines coming the banks way. I would not hesitate to say- do not work for lloyds if you value your sanity, work life balance and dignity... they`ll rip it all from you!!

headline: Experience differs greatly by group
pros:
Microsoft is really a dozen companies in one.  Pick carefully, and don't just think "Oh, it's Microsoft."  Groups differ greatly in their 

If you work for a really well-run group, then you can combine the benefits of working in a 200-person company with the benefits of working in an 80,000-person company.  The small group can give a sense of purpose and esprit de corps, while the large company can provide good healthcare, a stable environment, and lots of learning opportunities.  Go to the lunch tech talks to learn about the technology underlying other products.  Go to MSR to hear about what's going on in the blue-sky research world.

Flexible work hours, an informal attitude towards hierarchy.  And the ability to hop around between groups at Microsoft, to work on different technologies without the hassle of having to change companies.  If you get a sense of the landscape, know the landscape well, you can avoid most of the Cons listed below.
cons:
There is definitely a lot of dissatisfaction at upper management, especially Ballmer.  The recent layoffs have taken a toll on morale.  Again, it really differs by group.  Some groups got hit harder than others.

If you're in a bad group, you might get stuck in a death march for two years.  You might have coworkers who don't give a **** about the product and are just sitting around collecting checks.  But if you're in a good group, see Pros.

headline: No soul, direction or long term.....and they're the good points!!
pros:
Flexible working
Good facilities at locations refurbished to Vodafone standards
cons:
Where to start?:

Leadership and management - poor to non-existent, little of no regular feedback with direct manager, except where blame is to be attached to something that's not gone quite right.

Culture - Long hours and demands on personal time are off the scale. No appreciation of this, a VERY task driven, get it done, unrealistic demands on time/effort of people. There's no such thing as "bad news" being presented to Snr Managers and if it does, then junior people quickly take the flak, blame and responsibility for fixing "problems"

I've never come across an organisation with some many people feeling/looking stressed and completely maxed out with workload and demands on them.

A lot of interaction is on-line, calls, lync etc, so the "human" element of work is missing in very large parts, to the extent you could sit next to anyone on one of the campus for days and never know who they are or what they do, it's heads down and nothing social about people/teams.

Political - Power and connections are all in Vodafone. It's all about "perception" of you and not what you do, with Snr people quick and eager to take the credit for things going well and also VERY quick to seek blame when it doesn't

Support for people - It's a sink or swim mentality very quickly, after the good 3-day induction, you are thrown in and no leeway given for short time served. No-one seems to even care, or ask how new starters are getting on.

Constant Crisis management - Never think through initial decisions or project objectives and then apply extreme pressure and deadlines to fit original plan. Vodafone thrives on creating constant change, burning bridges, chaos and pressured work environment.

Organisation - completely silo and not joined up. Masses of work outsourced and makes it unfathomable to work through processes, if any exist at all. The C&W business still operates as a separate business and no sign of any organisational or cultural integration.

Culture in Tesco IT of breaking down employee by constant criticism/ reminding you that you can be easily replaced and that your paid too much money until employee leaves, is unable to leave and is kicked out or is moulded into one of their "yes" men/women. There was also a tendency for the managers to question your abilities and in some cases ask you to perform a tesk but will also ask a senior colleague to do the same task as they do not trust you. headline: Marketing
pros: Great compensation & benefits.  Lower level team members are great & hard working.  Good & a wide variety of experience gained quickly. Working from home has been a perk, but they are getting rid of that option for most positions.
cons:
Written by a millennial: Currently, IBM is going through a massive change where everyone has to relocate to one of 6 cities.  This tactic is to get rid of the middle/higher level positions who are older & cost more for employment & targeting millennials to hire in.  This tactic disregards the expertise that the mid-higher level employees have in their field.  Team members who have been at IBM for 20+ years are unappreciated & forced into retirement or to look for a new job. 

In addition to IBM not appreciating their loyal & experienced employees, IBM has a horrible onboarding process.  Starting as a university hire, I was just dropped in without understanding my role, the dynamics of the team, & was assigned as the lead for large events within the first couple months.  If IBM plans to replace all of the experienced IBM employees with new young hires, they need to have a very thorough training process in plan based on roles that has a single person assigned to the success of the new hire.

Lastly, all direction comes from the top down.  Reorganization happens at least once a year if not more.  The majority of the time people are trying to understand their role & new team members instead of being able to get quality work done.

headline: A once maternalistic company has become too American.  So sad :(
pros:
- Excellent Benefits including: Cash pension (instead of a traditional Def Benefit Plan), 401K matching, huge number of vacation days
- sometimes merit based
- gender equality (but not ethnic equality)
-meetings start late which gives a laid-back appearance
cons:
- Company has become too American, losing its soul in the process
- Company promotes ESG/CSR/Impact Investing but there is very little time for volunteer work
- very political with fiefdoms 
- difficult to move into other functions and teams
- top-down management style
- there's always a business restructuring regardless of whether they're needed
- too many meetings
-people waste each other's time
- no room for independent thinking (too much group think)
- decisions are usually made out of fear (of regulators, other teams, bosses, etc..)
- little cultural diversity
- no ethnic diversity
- the company claims its socially responsible, but no one has any visible disability that's working there
- too much pressure to work on periodic and ad hoc reports which few read
- very little time to think and be creative
- company is outsourcing as many functions as possible which lowers moral
- employees feel as if they're part of a corporate machine
- management has not taken enough responsibility for corporate misdeeds
- active age discrimination

headline: Revolving door policy reduces efficiency

A major issue is the unrealistic deadlines. Top management assigns unachievable assignments to temporary employees without training them, but employees are expected to devote their life to the project. When the project does not get done, the editors take are blamed, but the big picture is the the company lacks staff. When the morale is low and people's contracts are almost over, some employees stop working altogether.  Management has not figured out how to deal effectively with this problem.

headline: A Great Move

cons:
- Next have been supportive with WFH initiative, however it's quite director lead . 
- Standard holiday is on the lower side of average ( flexible with carry over and buildable over time and you can buy days, which helps)
- Teams very integrated and close (which is a pro, no doubt) however it would be nice for employees to have more regular one to ones for work (not necessarily development lead), than every 6 weeks, even once a month. 
- Although many not in office FT - it would be nice to see Next have more outdoor space for staff to take a break, as quite limited seating / areas.

headline: Expect to be treat like a slave
pros: Parking was nearly always available. And there is a metro 10 minutes walk away and buses right outside the building. I heavily suggest you drive, because if you miss your bus because of your 1pm-9pm shift, you're snookered.
cons:
- During training, they don't emphasise how important your monthly stats are. When you go into probation, you'll notice how strict your Team Leader is with this.
- Extra training is offerd, but really the QA department has different expectations for you. You will end up failing calls because of this and this will lead to a probation review and at worst, them ending your contract.
- Team Leaders have no time for you. They actually look at you like you're scum. Especially if you make them work for their earning. If you're looking to impress them, don't ask them vital question which will offer you tremendous expert knowledge. 
- Your wage doesn't account for the hours of work you do.  This industry is constantly hiring, a lot come in and a lot go out. That's the nature of it. Therefore expect a lot of annualised hours. I had this for 3 months doing 9 to 10 hours every day of the week I was in. 
- Book holidays 3 months in advance. I left it to a month before, they couldn't give me time off to attend my brother's wedding due the volume of customers in the dialler. 
- Was not given the day off after my car crash, to handle my insurance. Instead I had to try and resolve it in my 20 minute breaks. The matter was resolved 3 months later. Could've been resolved in 1-2 days. 
- Do not arrive back to your desk 0.001 seconds late for anything. You will be asked to go to a probation review. That's not a joke!

headline: Valet for food and Beverage
pros: Very upbeat and positive environment. The job allows you to experience all areas of the casino, including the card room, serving the gaming floor, behind the bar and in the restaurant. You are trained in all of these areas and shifts are alternated in different areas. Tips are great and the majority of people (most of the time) are friendly. there is almost always an opportunity to take up some overtime. There are also great incentives to work hard and a discount scheme for working at the casino. The also have free staff meals but if they do not take your fancy, you have 50% discount on food.
cons: Due to the casino being 24 hours, you would be required to work some late night or early morning shifts. Its important to remember that because its a casino there are always customers that may have lost money and therefore may be rude - but I found there is just equal amounts of rude people in the casino than there is in any food and beverage environment.

headline: Not worth the pay
pros: If you like working with people, you'll have fun. This is a relatively easy job when you have good co-workers.
cons: There aren't even benefits to make up for the low pay. Even when you work long shifts you rarely get a break. We never get holiday pay and apparently there is a new policy in place that only allows managers to get vacation pay. We also don't get raises often; I've only gotten one in three years here.

headline: Stable job
pros:
They will give you a permanent contract from the beginning, with a 3 month probation time. If your contract says 20 hours per week, you will never work less than that, unless you call in sick.

Shifts are fixed, which means you'll always have the same schedule, so you know how to plan the rest of the day. Depending on the floor and department you're working for, there is a pretty good chance you can get overtime hours too. And if the managers see that you're hardworking and eager to get more hours, they won't hesitate to call you when they'll need someone.

Tasks vary from day to day, even from hour to hour sometimes, which can be good, it keeps you moving, never getting too bored.

Managers are ok. They are usually nice people, understanding, easy to talk to.
cons:
AC is never on point. During summer, temperatures can go pretty high inside the store. And because you have to move all the time, tidying and attending to customers, maybe even serve at the tills, you'll obviously going to feel even warmer. And the uniforms don't help with that either, as they're only 33% cotton, which means you're going to sweat and you're not aloud to have any water on the shop floor!
During winters, it can get pretty cold. But if you learn to move fast enough and have a fleece with you just in case, you're ok.

Be ready to have all different kinds of customers, all in one day. They will not care how tidy your piles look or how neatly you've hanged the items, everything can end up on the floor in a matter of seconds.

There are no benefits or discounts for employees, except for 2 weeks in December, which I believe is not fair, considering we're being paid minimum wage.

Don't even consider applying for anything else within the company before you get one full year of experience with them on your current position. They won't let you change absolutely anything: you won't be able to ask for a different shift, try to apply for a promotion or a vacancy in a different department without a recommendation from your floor manager.

headline: Former Blue Harmony Team Lead
pros: The greatest thing about IBM is their work/life balance,  the benefits, the Teams I worked with and the Leadership of the Managers and Team Leads that I worked under.  The best in the business!  IBM was my dream job a long time before I was hired FT.  Before being hired FT with a once then reputable company, I was a contractor.  The IBM Management that I reported to saw my worth and treated me and others with respect.  When I was hired, it was a dream come true.  All my hard work finally paid off.  I worked with phenominal people who were patient, and compassionate with their peers and IBM.  At that time, IBM was doing quite well.  Life was great for all until 9/11/2001 happened.   Todays IBM may be different.  It may be growing in a more positive direction.
cons: When 9/11/2001 happened, it took a toll on IBM as well as other businesses.  The 9/11 affect was a slow trickle of poison to IBMs business.  However, I loved my job and the area I worked.  I thrived along with my peers.  Things were great until more and more resource actions were happening.  I was fortunate to be where I was until I decided to accept a job offer with no raise but a lateral move where the wind was blowing.  In the first year in this job, I worked the hardest I ever had in a job in my entire working life.  However, because I was at the bottom end of the totem pole I received an 'average' rating and NO raise.  My heartdropped and so did my morale.  I had two counterparts doing basically the same thing, and getting paid significantly more, but they were secure and up on the totem pole farther.   I knew then that I had two more years to go of being resourced.  That was the word in the hallways and it proved to be true.  Very disappointing in a company that I had dreamt of working for, for many years.   Before that, I excelled and received above average ratings and raises.  The fear of being resourced always lingered every time that dreaded news surfaced.   To me and others, this was just a way of resourcing employees.   Offer someone a new opportunity just so they can give you the shaft.  It did not matter how hard you worked.  It all depended on your placement on the totem pole.   So if you have been working in a job for a while, stay where you are at.  Do not leave.  I have applied for jobs at IBM since then, that I was very qualified for, but I am not given a fair chance.  I even had interviewed for a job before I was kicked out,  which I was positive I had, but the response I got was, 'We cannot hire you at this time'.   More disappointing news.   I believe I am flagged from being given an equal opportumity on jobs that I know I meet the qualifications.

headline: Good company, good salary, but...
pros:
It has an overall good salary, besides, they offer a pension scheme, private medical cover and a travel allowance or company car, depending on the nature of your role. Also, they give you up to 25 days holiday per year.

They also offer you training in your area of work. If you work there as an intern, you are assigned a mentor who is an expert in the area and who guides you during your internship.

Also, the work environment is very comfortable. My time at Balfour Beatty was very pleasant thanks to my work partners. I would say that it was the best part of working there.
cons: I felt like promotions and better job opportunities were given to people who didn't deserved them. Sometimes my work and my effort would go unnoticed.

headline: Great place to work
pros: A dynamic environment with lots of opportunities to develop. A collaborative environment with some of the best leadership in the business.
cons:
There is quite a rigid nature of promotions in the Legal & Compliance department that I felt was not ideal, particular at more junior levels. There is a formal annual process, combined with a lot of levels; Analyst, Associate, Manager, Director, VP, ED & MD.

Promotions are great and should be celebrated, but career progression in the form of going for a title change every year or two years isn't what most people want, and often at the junior levels the change in title doesn't meaningfully reflect a change in role.

headline: Arrogant culture, no collaboration, bullying management and not an equal opportunity employer
pros:
Good benefits, even though health care is becoming expensive. 
Amazing talks at Microsoft Research, mainly by invited guests. 
Huge company so there is the possibility to change roles quite easily. 
The pay is quite good especially because the main location is in WA. 
Great bus that brings you all over campus.
Some new cool Microsoft devices.
cons:
My main criticism is that it is not a company that values women in the workplace. I have not observed women getting promoted and women from technical fields are rarely being hired.  Teams remain disproportionately male even compared to recent statistics from Google and other competitors.  Little effort is made to proactively recruit women in technical positions. Managers from my division regularly and openly discredit women.  They bully them (from my personal experience). It feels like Anchorman over there. The maternity/parental benefits are BS. I took them and it cost me a poor review (I was doing great before and in all prior reviews).

If you are in my situation, know that a lot of tech companies are proactively hiring women, publishing their statistics as cause for change, and rewarding women for their good technical work.  The underlying belief in other companies is that women have equal capacity to be technical and do technical work.  This is not the case at Microsoft.  Credit is frequently given to male co-workers on the basis of their arrogance and posture. The presumption is that technical contributions in collaborations come from men. This is promoted by managers at many levels. For instance, women are frequently required to justify more of the technical claims on patents then their male counterparts on similar patents.  The presumed inequality is part of everyday engagements.

Real equal opportunity teams may exist in the company. Just be sure to interview your future Microsoft colleagues and understand the culture you are going into. I have excellent offers in other places now, and I am taking the one that best fits my personality and values. I know I will have a great impact there. I just wished I had moved out sooner from an environment that was toxic for my health. 

There is no collaboration possible, and unless you are a very vocal person and snarky to a fault, more arrogant people steal the credit for your work. I have struggled with this lack of collaboration and very competitive atmosphere. I was hoping that with the Nokia integration, a more collaborative culture would influence Microsoft leaders, but unfortunately it did not happen and all of those guys got fired anyway.

Regarding the interview process, the recruiter really tries to lowball you compared to other companies. This was my experience interviewing initially and then later interviewing internally.  Crap tactics like acting baffled and surprised at basic packages offered by many of their competitor companies are the norm.  Can they understand more collaborative techniques and work together on a good package? They do not reward PhDs.  They do not reward the caliber of the institution you're coming from. It is because most of the management lack graduate education and believe education is useless (I heard it many times at work). 

A minor thing, no free food, and food becomes quite expensive if you have only 20minutes to eat and need to eat in the local expensive cafeteria in the Commons (about 10-15$ a lunch). Something to budget.

The culture is really engraved in every manager who has been there from the start. Of course, you can find an excellent team (it does exist!) and if the work is a good fit, go for it. Be sure to probe the team's culture and get to know who will be your skip level.

headline: LinkedIn has been a Dream
pros: I came from the medical field and I have been extremely fortunate to have found a new home at LinkedIn. From the free food, to the amazing opportunities given on In Day, to the ultra fun and friendly staff; LinkedIn has far surpassed my expectations of what working in tech would be like. I have had more opportunities to learn and grow both professionally and personally in my short 6 months working here then I have ever had. The food is world class and delicious everyday. The Speaker Series that have been offered to the employees are so enriching and add so much value to my life. I even got to meet one of my heroes, Deepak Chopra. The culture is incredible and really makes LinkedIn very unique to other companies. They have an outstanding music program which I am honored to be a part of. I am even a member of the LinkedIn a capella group, InTune. It is such a blessing to be given the opportunity to preform with such incredibly smart and talented peers. All in all, LinkedIn has been the greatest place I have ever worked and I can't see myself ever wanting to seek employment anywhere else.
cons: The traffic getting to and from work. But, in the Bay Area, you can't really escape it.

headline: Great place to be from.
pros: Frequently a leader in technology development, though one's exposure and participation in that effort depends upon which part of the company you work for.  Most of the management is "sane", the real wackos are filtered out (usually).  The company has been around so long that they've seen everything and don't often panic when situations (of all kinds) occur.
cons: So large that the goals of different parts of the company often conflict.  Benefits, vacation, pension, stock purchase plan, medical, etc. have all been squeezed in recent years so the fringe benefits of working for IBM are not so great.  They're also working hard to transfer jobs out of the USA to China and India, this makes one's future with the company in the USA less stable.  The level of expertise of the technical talent can vary widely, many parts of the company are not attractive to really talented people who can get jobs elsewhere.

headline: McDonald's is an ok place to work if you don't mind working long hours and are good at sucking up to your boss.
pros: McDonald's is one of the few companies that is doing well in this weak economy.  The strong brand is a huge advantage for the company.  For those willing to work long hours, work exceptionally hard and be very agreeable with management, there are advancement opportunities.  Benefits are good.  The Oak Brook offices are comfortable and well maintained.
cons:
McDonald's is very ingrown and not very open to ideas from outsiders.   McDonald's values "nice" people over competent people.   It's extremely important to not "rock the boat" - management at all levels is not open to suggestions.  Success at McDonald's requires a lot of networking and office politics are very important.  This isn't a bad thing, it's just something to be aware of.  Knowing how the office game is played is important at every company but is especially important at McDonald's.  There's a certain type of person who succeeds at McDonald's - very cheerful, kind of bland, willing to work long hours without complaining, able to be extremely tactful.  Again, not all bad, just the way it is.  I think it's telling that year after year, most departments score low on the employee survey area asking whether "a free exchange of ideas is encouraged."  Many managers are of the mindset of "don't tell me any bad news."  

The other thing to remember about McDonald's is that it's a very large company.  There are small pockets of the company that do interesting and innovative work.  The Romeoville Innovation Center is a great place to work.  It doesn't seem to have the "play it safe" attitude that pervades much of McDonald's.  

Overall, the company is not bad but in most of the office jobs, it's much more important to get along with people than to be innovative.  Ideally, you want people who are both fun to work with and are great at their jobs and it's important that a company inspire a feeling of respect for all employees.  McDonald's does try to do this.  However, the social and networking aspects of the company and the need to "go along" are so important to an individual's success that someone who is at all outspoken will have a very difficult time here.

Everything I've said to this point has to do with professional jobs at McDonald's.  Restaurant jobs are boring and poorly paid.  Restaurant managers work extremely hard.  In many ways, the success of McDonald's is built on the backs of poorly paid overworked restaurant managers and crew.

headline: Great Job, Some down-sides
pros: Apple is one of the best companies I've ever worked for. They are human/people focused and foster and maintain some of the best company culture I've seen. The managers are (for the most part) good people who care about people, and the company as a whole, takes good care of the employees. As a part-time employee, I had full benefits and stock options. This is an IDEAL job for someone in college. It's a great way to start a 401k (and begin investing) and a great place to make friends.
cons: The work/life balance here is somewhat difficult because the nature of retail. Even part time people are expected to be available up to 28 hours a week, on nights, weekends, holidays, etc. The job requires a lot of standing, so be prepared for your legs, feet, back and hips to cry out in pain when you first start. Also, as with all retails jobs, be prepared to deal with rude,  angry, and  entitled customers all day.

headline: Good place to start your career
pros:
- Wide scope of work and range of clients. If you work really hard and prove your worth, there are a lot of great opportunities. If you put in the hard work, you can become an expert in the area that you choose to specialize in. 
- A lot of good people although as always in Big 4 many friends will leave in a few years 
- Good name on your resume
- working in the PwC network gives your lots of international exposure from the type of clients / work you do and with secondment opportunities
cons:
These comments apply to the Singapore firm 
- Dysfunctional office politics in the Singapore firm, especially in the past few years
- No work life balance for people who actually do work 
- Some teams in the Singapore firm tend to recognize people who can jockey their way up through politicking, but are not good team players (take credit from other's work) and are not technically strong in their area of specialization (which is bad for business because clients engage Big 4 for subject matter experts) 
- Insufficient onboarding and opportunities for mid-streamers and secondees. In some teams no real-time honest feedback are provided so many staff end up being underutilized. "Stars" are overloaded with work which is not sustainable
